summary: TypeScript makes it easier to read and debug JavaScript code.

questions.questionText: Which of the following does TypeScript use to specify types?
questions.explanation: TS uses a colon (:) to separate the property name from the property type

questions.questionText: Which of the following is NOT a type used in TypeScript?
questions.explanation: enum is not used as a type in TypeScript

questions.questionText: How can we specify properties and methods for an object in TypeScript?
questions.explanation: interfaces are typically used to list the properties and methods for an object

questions.questionText: How else can Array<number> be written in TypeScript?
questions.explanation: number[] is another way of writing Array<number> in TypeScript

questions.questionText: In which of these does a class take parameters?
questions.explanation: a constructor is used by a class to take in parameters

questions.questionText: Which is NOT an access modifier?
questions.explanation: async is not used as an access modifier type in TypeScript

questions.questionText: Which keyword allows us to share information between files in TypeScript?
questions.explanation: the export keyword allows for the information to be transmitted between files

questions.questionText: Which is an array method to generate a new array based on a condition?
questions.explanation: filter is a method used to conditionally create a new array

questions.questionText: How is a property accessible within a class?
questions.explanation: this.propertyName is the way to access a specific property within a class

summary: Angular allows us to create an app that contains components and modules as well as a system for bootstrapping the app.

questions.questionText: Which of the following are true statements about Angular?

options.text: Angular minimizes much of the code you would have to write.
Angular extends HTML's syntax to define your HTML components.

questions.explanation: all of the above are true statements about Angular

questions.questionText: Which of the following can be added to an Angular class?
questions.explanation: properties and methods can be added to a class in Angular

questions.questionText: How is a class adorned in Angular?
questions.explanation: a class is adorned with the @Component decorator in Angular

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of a decorator in TypeScript?

options.text: To attach metadata to a class, function, property or variable.
To add properties and methods to the class.
To minimize the amount of code you will write.

questions.explanation: a decorator attaches metadata to a class, function, property or variable

questions.questionText: Which defines the location of a component?
questions.explanation: a selector defines the location of a component in Angular

questions.questionText: Which defines the HTML code that the component generates?
questions.explanation: a template contains the HTML code that a component generates

questions.questionText: Which is used for grouping Angular building blocks together?
questions.explanation: the NgModule is used to group Angular components and modules together

questions.questionText: How is an Angular module adorned?
questions.explanation: @NgModule is used to decorate an Angular module

questions.questionText: Which array specifies components belonging to AppModule?
questions.explanation: the declarations array contains all the components that belong to AppModule

questions.questionText: Which of the following code is used for bootstrapping an app?
questions.explanation: passing AppModule into the platformBrowserDynamic() is the method used for bootstrapping an Angular app

summary: Angular has a very expressive template system, which takes HTML as a base, and extends it with custom elements.

questions.questionText: What characters are used for text interpolation?
questions.explanation: backticks are used in Angular for insertion of text

questions.questionText: Which characters are used to include a property value?
questions.explanation: double curlies are used to insert a property value inside a template

questions.questionText: How can you pass a value to a child element's attribute?
questions.explanation: properties can be based to a child element using string interpolation or [attribute]="property" syntax

questions.questionText: Which is a shortcut for applying a class name based on value of property?
questions.explanation: we use the [class.property] syntax to assign a class name based on a value of property.

questions.questionText: What is the proper way to bind styles to a button in Angular?
questions.explanation: [style.styleProperty] is the way of binding a style to an element in Angular

questions.questionText: Which of the following are examples of event bindings in Angular?
questions.explanation: (click) and its HTML equivalent 'on-click' are examples of event bindings

questions.questionText: How do we provide access to an HTML element or Angular component from the template?
questions.explanation: marking it with #name is the way to access the HTML element from an Angular template

questions.questionText: What mechanism does Angular provide for handling keyboard shortcuts?
questions.explanation: (keydown.control.enter) is Angular's syntax for handling keyboard shortcuts

questions.questionText: Which directive adds or removes an element from the DOM?
questions.explanation: ngIf is used to conditionally add or remove an element from the DOM

questions.questionText: Which directive can be used to display an array of cat images?
questions.explanation: the ngFor directive is used to display entire arrays or objects

summary: Dependency Injection is a way of providing dependencies in your code instead of hard-coding them.

questions.questionText: What is Dependency Injection (DI) in Angular?

options.text: A way to inject services into classes.

questions.explanation: it is Angular's way of injecting services into classes.

questions.questionText: What is the objective of dependency injection?

options.text: Pass the service to the client.
Allow the client to find service.
Allow the client to build service.

questions.explanation: a service gets passed to the client during DI

questions.questionText: Which of the following benefit from dependency injection?
questions.explanation: DI simplifies both programming and testing as well as being a popular design pattern

questions.questionText: Which of the following statements are true about Dependency Injection (DI)? Select all that apply.

options.text: DI is a technique where a class receives its dependencies from external sources rather than creating them itself.
DI always leads to significant performance improvements in applications.
DI helps in reducing the coupling between classes, making the code more modular.
DI is only applicable for high-level modules and cannot be used with low-level modules.

questions.explanation: DI involves a class receiving dependencies from external sources and reduces coupling between classes, enhancing modularity.

questions.questionText: In which of the following does dependency injection occur?
questions.explanation: object instantiations are taken care of by the constructor in Angular

questions.questionText: What is the first step in setting up dependency injection?
questions.explanation: the first step is marking the class as @Injectable()

summary: An Angular application can be thought of as a tree of reusable components.

questions.questionText: What is a component tree in Angular?

options.text: A visual representation of how components are nested within an application
A data structure used to store component metadata
A tool for debugging Angular applications
A lifecycle hook used to initialize components

questions.explanation: a component tree in Angular shows how components are nested and related

questions.questionText: Why is understanding the component tree important in Angular development?

options.text: It helps in managing application styles.
It assists in debugging and understanding component interactions.
It is used to configure Angular modules.
It determines the order of service injection.

questions.explanation: knowing the component tree aids in troubleshooting and clarifying how components relate to each other

questions.questionText: How does a parent component pass data to its child component?
questions.explanation: a parent component can pass data to its child via properties

questions.questionText: How can one component render another one?

options.text: Using an HTML element that matches selector of other component.

questions.explanation: a component can display another component by matching the selector of the other component

questions.questionText: How do components know about each other?

options.text: If they are declared in the same module.
If they are passed to each other.

questions.explanation: components can only know about each other if they are declared in the same module

questions.questionText: How must a child decorate its properties to pass data?
questions.explanation: properties must be decorated with @Input() in a child in order to pass data to its parent

summary: Angular Router helps developers build Single Page Applications with multiple views and allow navigation between those views.

questions.questionText: Which is used to assign URLs to different parts of your app?
questions.explanation: Router is used to provide URLs to the different parts of your app.

questions.questionText: How are routes configured in Angular?

options.text: By defining an array of mapping between URL path and a component.
When the app is being bootstrapped.

questions.explanation: routes are configured by defining an array of mapping between URL path and a component

questions.questionText: How does Angular know about the routing configuration?

options.text: We have to pass the config to our module.
When the app is being bootstrapped.
It looks for the config in router-outlet.

questions.explanation: routes are configured by defining an array of mapping between URL path and a component

questions.questionText: Which of the following creates an Angular module from our configuration?
questions.explanation: RouterModule.forRoot establishes an Angular module from the routing configuration

questions.questionText: What tag is used to tell the router where to display a selected component?
questions.explanation: the router-outlet tag tells the router to display selected component(s)

questions.questionText: Which directive is used to provide a URL in creating a route?
questions.explanation: the routerLink directive is used to provide the URL necessary for routing

summary: Angular Material provides a set of Material Design components that are consistent, versatile and look great on mobile devices.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is the official website for Angular Material?
questions.explanation: the official website for Angular Material is material.angular.io

questions.questionText: Where are Angular Material modules declared in NgModule?
questions.explanation: the imports section of NgModule is where Angular Material modules are declared

questions.questionText: Which of the following is true about Angular Material Design components?
questions.explanation: all of these are true about Angular Material components.

questions.questionText: Where are Angular Material components used?
questions.explanation: Angular Material components are utilized in the template file

questions.questionText: Which tag can be used to display a card header in Angular Material?
questions.explanation: the mat-card-header is used to display the header information inside an Angular mat-card

questions.questionText: Which tag can be used to display actions in Angular Material?
questions.explanation: the mat-card-actions tag is used to display actions inside an Angular Material template

summary: Angular forms build upon standard HTML forms to help create custom form controls and support easy validation.

questions.questionText: What is the first step in using Angular Forms?

options.text: Add form tag to the template.
Add form fields in the template.
Add form validation fields to the TypeScript file.

questions.explanation: we must first add FormsModule to the imports section of NgModule

questions.questionText: What syntax is used to bind inputs to fields on the component?
questions.explanation: the "banana-in-a-box" syntax [(ngModel]] is used for binding inputs to fields

questions.questionText: How do you provide validation for your inputs?
questions.explanation: by placing the "required" keyword on an input field, it marks the field for validation

questions.questionText: Which of the following are steps to display a validation error?
questions.explanation: using #name="ngModel" and using usernameModel's error property are the two steps to display a validation error

questions.questionText: Which is NOT a built-in validator that Angular provides?
questions.explanation: password is not a built-in validator which Angular provides

questions.questionText: Which is true if the user changed the value of the input?
questions.explanation: dirty is true when the user changes the value of the input

questions.questionText: Which is true if the user focused on the input and then blurred without changing the value?
questions.explanation: touched is true when the user focuses on input and then blurred

questions.questionText: Which of the following wraps the input in an Angular Material form?
questions.explanation: mat-form-field surrounds the input in an Angular Material form

questions.questionText: How is an error shown in an Angular Material form?
questions.explanation: the mat-error tag is used to show an error in an Angular Material form

summary: The Angular CLI is a command-line interface tool used for initializing, developing, scaffolding and maintaining Angular applications.

questions.questionText: Which is a prerequisite for using Angular-CLI?
questions.explanation: NodeJS needs to be installed on your machine before setting up Angular-CLI

questions.questionText: How is Angular-CLI installed?
questions.explanation: Angular-CLI is installed using the "npm install -g @angular/cli" command

questions.questionText: How can you run an app that is built with Angular-CLI?
questions.explanation: ng serve is the command that is required to run an app built with Angular-CLI

questions.questionText: Which URL should be viewed in the browser to see the app running?
questions.explanation: viewing http://localhost:4200/ allows us to view the app in the browser

questions.questionText: What command is used to create a new service using the Angular-CLI?
questions.explanation: a service is created using the ng g s shortcut command

questions.questionText: Which is a true statement about the Angular CLI?

options.text: It is a command-line tool that makes it easy to generate new components.
It allows for fast scaffolding of an application that works easily.
It takes care of the build system for you.

questions.explanation: all of the above are true statements about the Angular-CLI

summary: Change detection keeps the view in sync with your data — learn how it runs, the OnPush strategy, and how to optimize it.

questions.questionText: What is the primary job of Angular's change detection?

options.text: Keep the view (DOM) in sync with the component's data model.

questions.explanation: Change detection keeps the rendered view (DOM) in sync with the component's data model whenever that data changes.

questions.questionText: Which library does Angular use by default to know WHEN to run change detection?
questions.explanation: Zone.js patches async APIs (DOM events, timers, XHR) so Angular knows when something may have changed and runs change detection.

questions.questionText: Which change detection strategy checks a component only when an @Input reference changes, an event fires inside it, or an async-pipe observable emits?
questions.explanation: ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush limits checks to new @Input references, events originating in the component, and async-pipe emissions.

questions.questionText: With OnPush, why must you pass a NEW object or array reference instead of mutating the existing one?

options.text: OnPush compares @Input values by reference, so an in-place mutation keeps the same reference and is missed.
OnPush deep-clones every input on each cycle.
OnPush only works with primitive inputs.

questions.explanation: OnPush uses reference equality for @Input. Mutating an object or array in place keeps the same reference, so the change goes undetected — provide a new reference instead.

questions.questionText: Which ChangeDetectorRef method marks a component and its ancestors to be checked on the next cycle (often used with OnPush after an async update)?
questions.explanation: markForCheck() flags the component and its path to the root as dirty so they are checked on the next change detection run.

questions.questionText: Which of the following trigger Angular's change detection by default? Select all that apply.

options.text: A DOM event such as a click

questions.explanation: Zone.js patches DOM events, XHR, and timers, so each triggers change detection. Code inside runOutsideAngular() intentionally escapes the zone and does NOT trigger it.

questions.questionText: In a zoneless Angular app (no Zone.js), what does Angular rely on to know when to update the view?

options.text: Signals and explicit notifications (e.g. signal reads, async pipe, markForCheck)

questions.explanation: Zoneless Angular schedules change detection from signals and explicit notifications instead of relying on Zone.js to patch async APIs.

questions.questionText: Which are valid ways to optimize change detection performance?

options.text: Use the OnPush change detection strategy.
Use immutable data so inputs get new references.
Use the async pipe or signals instead of manual subscriptions.

questions.explanation: OnPush, immutable data (new references), and the async pipe or signals all reduce unnecessary change detection work.

summary: Talk to servers the modern Angular way — provideHttpClient, typed requests, params and headers, error handling, functional interceptors, and cancellation.
facts: By default HttpClient parses the response body as JSON — set responseType to 'text', 'blob', or 'arraybuffer' when you need something else.
HttpParams and HttpHeaders are immutable — methods like set() and append() return a new instance instead of mutating the original.
The HttpClient testing tools (provideHttpClientTesting and HttpTestingController) let you unit-test HTTP calls without making real network requests.

questions.questionText: In a standalone Angular 20 app, how do you make HttpClient available for injection?

options.text: Add provideHttpClient() to the providers passed to bootstrapApplication().
Import HttpClientModule into every standalone component.
Create it yourself with new HttpClient() wherever it is needed.
Assign HttpClient to a global window variable at startup.

questions.explanation: provideHttpClient() is added to the app's providers (in bootstrapApplication or appConfig) to make HttpClient injectable. HttpClientModule is the older, discouraged setup, and you never construct HttpClient yourself.

questions.questionText: Which snippet correctly obtains HttpClient inside a standalone service?
questions.explanation: HttpClient is obtained through Angular's DI — most concisely with inject(HttpClient), or via constructor injection. You never create it with new, and HttpClient.get() is not a static factory.

questions.questionText: Which of the following statements about HTTP method semantics are correct? Select all that apply.

options.text: GET retrieves data and should not change server state.
POST is typically used to create a new resource.
PUT replaces a resource, while PATCH applies a partial update.
DELETE is used to fetch a resource without removing it.

questions.explanation: GET is safe and read-only, POST creates resources, and PUT replaces a resource while PATCH updates it partially. DELETE removes a resource — it does not fetch one.

questions.questionText: How do you tell HttpClient to type the response body as a User object?
questions.explanation: HttpClient methods take a generic type, so http.get<User>(url) returns an Observable<User> with a typed body. It is a compile-time generic — there is no runtime type argument or getTyped() method.

questions.questionText: Which is the correct way to add the query parameter ?page=2 to a GET request?
questions.explanation: query parameters go in the params option, e.g. { params: new HttpParams().set('page', 2) }, which Angular serializes to ?page=2. There is no query option or setParam() method.

questions.questionText: How do you attach an Authorization header to a single POST request?

options.text: this.http.post('/api/items', body, { headers: new HttpHeaders().set('Authorization', token) })

questions.explanation: per-request headers go in the headers option using HttpHeaders, e.g. { headers: new HttpHeaders().set('Authorization', token) }. For a header on every request, a functional interceptor is the better choice.

questions.questionText: Which statements about handling HttpClient errors with catchError() are correct? Select all that apply.

options.text: catchError() is placed inside the observable's pipe() to intercept a failed request.
Inside catchError() you can return a fallback observable such as of([]) to recover gracefully.
Re-throwing with throwError(() => err) lets the error propagate to the subscriber.
catchError() automatically retries the failed request three times.

questions.explanation: catchError() is an RxJS operator used inside pipe() to handle a failed request. You can recover by returning a fallback such as of([]) or re-throw with throwError(() => err); it does not retry on its own.

questions.questionText: In Angular 20, functional HTTP interceptors are plain functions registered with withInterceptors() (passed to provideHttpClient()) that can modify both outgoing requests and incoming responses. True or False?
questions.explanation: functional interceptors are plain functions — (req, next) => next(req) — registered with withInterceptors() in provideHttpClient(). They transform the outgoing request and incoming response, replacing the class-based HTTP_INTERCEPTORS.

questions.questionText: A search box fires a GET request on each keystroke. Which approach best cancels the previous in-flight request when a new keystroke arrives?

options.text: Pipe the keystrokes through switchMap() into the HttpClient call, so a new emission unsubscribes from the previous request and Angular aborts it if it is still in flight.
Call request.cancel() on the object returned by http.get().
Pass { cancelPrevious: true } in the request options.
Nothing can be done — an HTTP request can always be cancelled retroactively once it has been sent.

questions.explanation: switchMap() unsubscribes from the previous request when a new value arrives, aborting it only if it is still in flight. There is no request.cancel() or cancelPrevious option, and an already-completed request cannot be undone.

questions.questionText: Which are recommended best practices for using HttpClient in Angular 20? Select all that apply.

options.text: Encapsulate HTTP calls in dedicated services instead of calling HttpClient from components.
Use typed responses and handle failures with catchError().
Use functional interceptors for cross-cutting concerns like auth headers and logging.
Subscribe to the same request observable in many places to reuse a single network call.

questions.explanation: wrap HTTP calls in services, use typed responses with catchError(), and put cross-cutting concerns in interceptors. Subscribing to the same observable in many places does not reuse one call (they are cold) — use shareReplay() or the async pipe.

summary: Test Angular the modern way — TestBed and fixtures, change detection, injected services, spies and mocks, async, signals, HTTP, and choosing the right test level.
facts: Playwright tests run through a real browser context, while Angular Jest unit tests run in a faster, more isolated jsdom environment.
Excessive mocking can make a test pass even when the real, integrated behavior is broken — so mock only what a test genuinely needs to isolate.
TestBed builds a dedicated testing module for each test, keeping tests isolated from the real application bootstrap and from each other.

questions.questionText: You want to verify a single pure function that formats a score, in isolation and as fast as possible. Which type of test is the best fit?

options.text: An end-to-end (Playwright) test that clicks through the UI.
A manual test in the browser each time.
An integration test spanning several components and services.

questions.explanation: a unit test verifies a small, isolated piece of logic like a pure function — fast and without spinning up the whole app. End-to-end tests exercise real user flows and are slower; pick the lowest level that gives useful confidence.

questions.questionText: What is the role of TestBed.configureTestingModule({ ... }) in an Angular test?

options.text: It sets up the testing module — the standalone imports, declarations, and providers the test needs.
It launches a real browser to run the test.

questions.explanation: TestBed.configureTestingModule() sets up the testing module — the imports, declarations, and providers a test needs — before anything is created. It does not launch a browser, build for production, or provide assertions (that is Jest's expect()).

questions.questionText: After configuring TestBed, how do you create a component and get access to it for assertions?

options.text: const fixture = TestBed.createComponent(MyComponent), then read fixture.componentInstance.

questions.explanation: TestBed.createComponent(MyComponent) returns a ComponentFixture: fixture.componentInstance is the component and fixture.nativeElement is its rendered DOM. Using new bypasses DI and rendering, and TestBed.inject is for services.

questions.questionText: A test updates a component's data, then asserts the rendered text in fixture.nativeElement — but the DOM still shows the old value. What is the most likely fix?

options.text: Construct the component again with new.
Call fixture.detectChanges() to run change detection before asserting the DOM.
Read from the production DOM instead of nativeElement.

questions.explanation: Angular does not auto-render on every change in a test, so call fixture.detectChanges() to run change detection and update the DOM before asserting on fixture.nativeElement. Creating a fixture does not guarantee every cycle runs for you.

questions.questionText: You are testing a ScoreService that depends on other providers. What is the recommended way to obtain it in a Jest + TestBed test?

options.text: const svc = new ScoreService(); with no providers configured.
Import the instance from the component under test.
Read it off the global window object.
Register providers in TestBed, then const svc = TestBed.inject(ScoreService);

questions.explanation: when a service's dependencies or providers matter, get it through DI: register providers in TestBed and retrieve it with TestBed.inject(ScoreService). Using new bypasses DI — direct construction only fits a dependency-free class.

questions.questionText: Which statements about test doubles in this Jest-based project are correct? Select all that apply.

options.text: jest.fn() creates a mock function whose calls and arguments you can assert.
jest.spyOn(obj, 'method') wraps an existing method so you can observe or override it.
A stub supplies canned return values to keep a test focused and deterministic.
jasmine.createSpyObj() is the standard way to create spies in this project.

questions.explanation: this project uses Jest, so jest.fn() creates assertable mock functions, jest.spyOn() wraps existing methods, and stubs return canned values for determinism. jasmine.createSpyObj() belongs to Jasmine, which this project does not use.

questions.questionText: Which are valid ways to test asynchronous code in an Angular + Jest test? Select all that apply.

options.text: Use async/await with a promise-returning API.
Use fakeAsync() with tick() to advance virtual time for timers.
Assume the assertion runs after the async work with no awaiting or flushing.
Await fixture.whenStable() to let pending async work settle before asserting.

questions.explanation: async/await handles promise-based work, fakeAsync() with tick() advances virtual time for timers, and fixture.whenStable() waits for pending async tasks before you assert. Asserting with no awaiting or flushing checks state before the work finishes.

questions.questionText: Which are good practices when testing a component that uses Angular Signals? Select all that apply.

options.text: Reach into and directly overwrite the component's private signals from the test.
Trigger the state change the test needs, then read the resulting signal or computed value.
Call fixture.detectChanges() before asserting on the rendered DOM.
Assert the externally observable state or output rather than a signal's internal details.

questions.explanation: test Signals by driving the required state change and reading the resulting value or rendered output, running change detection before DOM assertions. Focus on observable behavior — mutating private signals couples the test to implementation details.

questions.questionText: Using Angular's HTTP testing utilities with HttpTestingController, what is the correct order to test a GET request?

options.text: Flush a mock response first, then make the request.
Allow a real network request, then assert the parsed body.
Make the request, match it with expectOne() and assert it, then flush() the mock response and verify().
Call fixture.detectChanges() to send the HTTP request.

questions.explanation: with HttpTestingController you trigger the request, match it with expectOne() (asserting method and URL), then flush() a mock response and call verify(). Unit tests must never hit the real network, and you assert the request before flushing.

questions.questionText: True or False: Playwright end-to-end tests are the best choice for verifying every small service method and individual component in isolation.
questions.explanation: Playwright runs real-browser end-to-end tests — ideal for critical user flows, routing, and cross-page behavior, but slower than unit tests. Small, isolated logic belongs in fast Jest unit tests; choose the lowest practical test level that gives useful confidence.

summary: Build faster Angular apps — change detection and OnPush, signals, @for track, lazy loading, @defer, image and template optimization, and smaller bundles.
facts: Hydration lets a server-rendered Angular app become interactive without re-rendering the whole page, reducing flicker and work on load.
Zoneless change detection (dropping Zone.js) lets Angular schedule updates from signals, cutting the overhead of patched async APIs.
Standalone components with the modern esbuild-based builder produce smaller, faster builds than legacy NgModule setups.

questions.questionText: Why does front-end performance matter for an Angular application?

options.text: It only matters for the development build, not production.
It affects TypeScript compile time but not the end user.
Faster load and response times improve user experience, engagement, and conversion.
It changes the app's business logic and features.

questions.explanation: performance directly shapes the user experience — faster startup and interaction keep users engaged and improve conversion. It is a runtime, user-facing concern, and optimizing it does not change the app's features.

questions.questionText: How does the OnPush change detection strategy help performance?

options.text: It disables change detection entirely for the whole app.
It skips checking a component unless an @Input reference changes, an event fires in it, or an async pipe emits.
It guarantees every app becomes faster with no trade-offs.
It re-renders the component on every browser animation frame.

questions.explanation: OnPush limits a component's checks to new @Input references, events inside it, and async-pipe emissions, so Angular skips large parts of the tree. It is not a guaranteed win — it relies on immutable data, so mutating an object in place can cause a missed update.

questions.questionText: Which statements about Angular Signals and performance are correct? Select all that apply.

options.text: Signals replace RxJS for all asynchronous streams and HTTP.
A signal notifies only the consumers that read it, enabling fine-grained updates.
computed() derives a value that recalculates only when its signal dependencies change.
Signals make change detection more targeted than checking whole component trees.

questions.explanation: a signal notifies only the consumers that read it, and computed() recalculates lazily when its dependencies change — far more targeted than checking whole trees. But signals model synchronous state and do not replace RxJS for streams like HTTP.

questions.questionText: In a large @for list that updates often, what does a track expression (e.g. @for (item of items; track item.id)) do for performance?

options.text: It lets Angular match items by identity and reuse existing DOM instead of recreating every row.
It sorts the list automatically on each change.
It caches the list in localStorage.
It disables change detection for the list.

questions.explanation: a track expression gives each item a stable identity, so Angular re-uses and moves existing DOM nodes instead of recreating every row when the array changes. Without it, a new array reference can force Angular to rebuild the whole list.

questions.questionText: What is the main performance benefit of lazy loading a feature route with loadComponent or loadChildren?

options.text: It makes every subsequent navigation instant.
It removes the need for a production build.
It eliminates change detection for that feature.
It keeps that feature's code out of the initial bundle, so the app starts faster.

questions.explanation: lazy loading splits a feature's code into a separate chunk that downloads on demand, shrinking the initial bundle and speeding up startup. It does not make later navigations instant — the chunk still downloads the first time it is needed.

questions.questionText: What does wrapping a heavy component in @defer (on viewport) { ... } do?

options.text: It renders the component immediately but hides it with CSS.
It delays loading and rendering that component until it enters the viewport, improving initial render.
It runs the component on a web worker.
It permanently disables the component's change detection.

questions.explanation: @defer postpones loading and rendering its content until a trigger fires (here, entering the viewport), so heavy or below-the-fold content does not block the first paint and its code can be split out. It defers real work, not just hides it.

questions.questionText: How do you optimize images in Angular 20 using the built-in directive?

options.text: Add loading='eager' to every img so they all load up front.
Inline every image as a base64 data URL in the template.
Use NgOptimizedImage with the ngSrc attribute to enable lazy loading, sizing, and priority hints.
Replace all images with CSS background-image for speed.

questions.explanation: NgOptimizedImage (via the ngSrc attribute) adds lazy loading, prevents layout shift by requiring width and height, and supports a priority hint for the LCP image. Loading every image eagerly or inlining base64 URLs bloats the initial payload.

questions.questionText: Which practices help avoid unnecessary template work during change detection? Select all that apply.

options.text: Move expensive calculations out of the template into a computed() or a memoized value.
Put complex getters in bindings so they recompute on every change detection cycle.
Avoid calling functions that do heavy work directly in template bindings.
Use the async pipe or signals so values update only when the data changes.

questions.explanation: template expressions run on every change detection cycle, so heavy functions or getters in bindings repeat that work constantly. Precompute with computed(), keep heavy functions out of bindings, and use the async pipe or signals so work happens only when the data changes.

questions.questionText: Which of these reduce an Angular app's bundle size? Select all that apply.

options.text: Building with the production configuration so tree shaking and minification run.
Code splitting via lazy-loaded routes and @defer.
Removing unused code and dependencies (dead-code elimination).
Importing a whole utility library just to use a single function.

questions.explanation: production builds enable tree shaking and minification, lazy routes and @defer split code into on-demand chunks, and removing unused code trims the payload. Importing a whole library for one function does the opposite, pulling in dead weight.

questions.questionText: True or False: You should measure and profile an app's performance to find the real bottlenecks before optimizing, rather than guessing.
questions.explanation: measuring with the browser profiler, Lighthouse, or Angular DevTools shows where time is actually spent, so you fix real bottlenecks instead of guessing. Premature optimization from assumptions adds complexity without improving the metrics that matter.

summary: Think reactively in Angular — Observables and operators, subjects, combining streams, error handling, and preventing memory leaks with the async pipe and takeUntil.
facts: Cold Observables (like HttpClient calls) start a fresh execution for each subscriber, while hot Observables (like a Subject) share one execution among all subscribers.
shareReplay() lets multiple subscribers share a single underlying execution and replays the last result, avoiding duplicate HTTP requests.
RxJS operators are pure, pipeable functions that return a new Observable, so unused operators are tree-shaken out of the production bundle.

questions.questionText: What best describes an Observable in RxJS?

options.text: A value that is computed once and stored synchronously.
A lazy stream of values over time that does nothing until you subscribe.
A JavaScript Promise with a few extra methods.
A global event bus shared across the whole app.

questions.explanation: an Observable is a lazy stream of values over time — it does nothing until you subscribe, then emits zero or more values, an error, or completion. Unlike a Promise (eager, single value, not cancellable), it is lazy, can emit many values, and is cancellable; it is not a stored value or a global event bus.

questions.questionText: You have an array [1, 2, 3]. Which creation function emits each element as a separate value?
questions.explanation: from() turns an array (or Promise/iterable) into an Observable that emits each element in turn — 1, then 2, then 3. of([1,2,3]) emits the whole array as one value, while interval and timer produce time-based emissions and do not take an array of values.

questions.questionText: Which pipe keeps only the even numbers and then doubles them?
questions.explanation: operators in pipe() run in order, so filter drops the odd numbers first and map then doubles the survivors. Option 2 doubles first, making every value even, so the filter removes nothing; tap only runs side effects and cannot filter; and switchMap must return an Observable, not a number.

questions.questionText: What is the correct use of the tap() operator?

options.text: To transform each emitted value into a new value.
To flatten an inner Observable into the outer stream.
To perform a side effect (like logging) without changing the emitted values.

questions.explanation: tap() runs a side effect — logging, debugging, updating something external — for each emission while passing the value through unchanged. Transforming values is map(), removing values is filter(), and flattening an inner Observable is a higher-order operator such as switchMap or mergeMap.

questions.questionText: Which statements about RxJS Subjects are correct? Select all that apply.

options.text: A Subject is both an Observable and an Observer, so it can multicast values to many subscribers.
A plain Subject replays its last value to any subscriber that arrives late.
A BehaviorSubject requires an initial value and gives new subscribers the current value immediately.
A ReplaySubject can re-emit a buffer of previous values to new subscribers.

questions.explanation: a Subject is both an Observable and an Observer, so it multicasts to many subscribers; a BehaviorSubject holds a current value (needing an initial one) and hands it to new subscribers immediately; and a ReplaySubject buffers and re-emits past values. A plain Subject does NOT replay — a late subscriber only receives values emitted after it subscribes.

questions.questionText: Why is the async pipe (data$ | async) usually preferred over manually subscribing in a component?

options.text: It makes the Observable emit its values faster.
It subscribes for you and automatically unsubscribes when the component is destroyed, preventing leaks.
It converts the Observable into a Promise.
It caches the last value in localStorage.

questions.explanation: the async pipe subscribes to the Observable, renders each emitted value, and automatically unsubscribes when the component is destroyed — removing a common memory-leak source and a lot of boilerplate. It does not speed up emissions, turn the Observable into a Promise, or persist values to localStorage.

questions.questionText: A type-ahead search fires an HTTP request on each keystroke. Which operator cancels the previous in-flight request when a new keystroke arrives?
questions.explanation: switchMap unsubscribes from the previous inner Observable when a new value arrives, cancelling the outdated request — ideal for type-ahead search. mergeMap runs every request concurrently with no cancellation, concatMap queues them in order, and exhaustMap ignores new input until the current request finishes.

questions.questionText: Which statements about combining Observables are correct? Select all that apply.

options.text: forkJoin waits for all source Observables to complete, then emits their last values once.
combineLatest emits an array of the latest values whenever any source emits.
zip pairs values by index, emitting only when every source has produced the next item.
forkJoin emits a new combined value every time any single source emits.

questions.explanation: forkJoin is like Promise.all — it waits for every source to complete and emits their final values once, great for firing several HTTP requests in parallel; combineLatest emits the latest values whenever any source emits; and zip pairs values by index. forkJoin does NOT emit continuously — that behavior belongs to combineLatest.

questions.questionText: Which are correct ways to handle errors and prevent memory leaks with RxJS in Angular? Select all that apply.

options.text: Rely on Angular to automatically unsubscribe from every manual .subscribe() call.
Use catchError to intercept an error and return a fallback Observable such as of([]).
Use takeUntil(this.destroy$) so subscriptions complete when the component is destroyed.
Prefer the async pipe so subscription and cleanup are handled for you.

questions.explanation: catchError intercepts a failed stream and lets you recover by returning a fallback Observable; takeUntil(destroy$) completes subscriptions when the component is destroyed; and the async pipe subscribes and unsubscribes automatically. Angular does NOT auto-unsubscribe from manual .subscribe() calls — leaving them open is a common memory-leak source.

questions.questionText: True or False: Nesting .subscribe() calls inside one another is the recommended way to chain dependent Observable requests in Angular.
questions.explanation: nested subscriptions are hard to read, error-prone, and leak easily. The reactive approach chains dependent requests with a higher-order operator like switchMap (or concatMap), keeping a single subscription; that also cancels stale inner requests and lets the async pipe manage cleanup.

summary: Master reactive state in Angular — writable signals, computed derived state, effects, signals in templates, and when to reach for signals instead of RxJS.
facts: input() creates a signal-based component input that replaces the @Input decorator and is read like any other signal in templates and computed values.
toSignal() and toObservable() bridge RxJS and Signals, so you can adopt Signals incrementally alongside existing Observable code.
In a zoneless Angular app, signals are the primary way Angular knows when to update the view, replacing Zone.js-based change detection.

questions.questionText: What is an Angular Signal?

options.text: A one-time value that never changes after it is created.
An RxJS Observable with a different name.
A reactive wrapper around a value that notifies its consumers when the value changes.
A directive that updates the DOM directly.

questions.explanation: a signal is a reactive wrapper around a value: you read it by calling it like a function, and when its value changes it notifies everything that depends on it — computed signals, templates, and effects. It is not a fixed constant, it is not an Observable (signals are synchronous and always hold a current value), and it is not a directive.

questions.questionText: How do you create a writable signal holding the number 0, and then read its value?

options.text: const count = signal(0); then read it with count().
const count = signal(0); then read it with count.value.
const count = new Signal(0); then read it with count.get().
const count = computed(() => 0); then read it with count().

questions.explanation: signal(0) creates a writable signal with an initial value, and you read it by calling it as a function: count(). Signals are not read with .value or .get(), you do not construct them with new, and computed() creates a read-only derived signal rather than a writable one.

questions.questionText: You have count = signal(0) and want to increment it based on its current value. Which is the idiomatic way?
questions.explanation: update() receives the current value and returns the new one, so count.update(n => n + 1) is the idiomatic way to derive the next value from the old. set() replaces the value outright and fits when the new value does not depend on the old (e.g. count.set(5)); reassigning the variable or using .value is not how signals work.

questions.questionText: You have firstName and lastName signals. How do you create a fullName that stays in sync automatically?
questions.explanation: computed() creates a read-only signal that re-derives its value whenever any signal it reads changes, so fullName updates automatically. A plain signal(...) captures the value once and won't track changes, a bare expression is a one-time string, and effect() runs side effects and does not return a usable value.

questions.questionText: Which statements about computed() signals are correct? Select all that apply.

options.text: A computed signal automatically tracks the signals it reads and recomputes when they change.
A computed signal recalculates on every change detection cycle, even if its dependencies did not change.
A computed signal is read-only; you cannot call set() or update() on it.
A computed signal is lazy and memoized — it recomputes only when it is read after a dependency changed.

questions.explanation: a computed signal tracks whichever signals it reads and recomputes when they change; it is read-only (no set/update); and it is lazy and memoized, recomputing only when read after a dependency changed. It does NOT recompute on every change detection cycle — that memoization is exactly what avoids wasted work.

questions.questionText: What is the primary purpose of effect()?

options.text: To derive a new value from other signals.
To run a side effect (like logging or syncing to localStorage) whenever the signals it reads change.
To create a new writable signal.
To manually trigger Angular change detection.

questions.explanation: effect() runs a side effect — logging, syncing to storage, DOM work outside bindings — automatically whenever any signal it reads changes. Deriving a value is computed()'s job (prefer it over an effect that just sets another signal), creating state is signal(), and effects are not how you trigger change detection.

questions.questionText: How do you display a signal's value in a template, and what does Angular do when it changes?

options.text: {{ count.value }} — Angular polls the signal on a timer.
{{ count | async }} — the async pipe subscribes to the signal.
{{ count() }} — Angular tracks the read and updates just that view when the signal changes.
{{ count }} — Angular renders the signal object itself.

questions.explanation: in the template you call the signal like a function, {{ count() }}, and Angular records that this view depends on it, so when it changes only the affected part of the view updates — no async pipe or manual subscription needed. Signals are not read with .value, the async pipe is for Observables, and rendering the bare signal would show the function, not its value.

questions.questionText: Which statements comparing Signals and RxJS are correct? Select all that apply.

options.text: A signal always holds a current value you can read synchronously, similar to a BehaviorSubject's current value.
Signals suit synchronous UI state, while RxJS excels at async streams and event pipelines.
Reading a signal needs no subscribe/unsubscribe, avoiding that class of memory leak.
Signals fully replace RxJS for HTTP requests and complex async coordination.

questions.explanation: a signal always has a current value you can read synchronously (like a BehaviorSubject), it fits synchronous UI state while RxJS shines for async streams, and reading it needs no subscription so there is no unsubscribe leak. Signals do NOT replace RxJS for HTTP or complex async work — those still belong to Observables, often bridged with toSignal().

questions.questionText: Which are recommended practices when working with Angular Signals? Select all that apply.

options.text: Put heavy work and side effects inside computed() so the UI stays in sync.
Keep computed() functions pure — no side effects and no mutating other signals.
Prefer computed() over an effect() when you only need to derive a value.
Model local component state with signals instead of manual change detection.

questions.explanation: computed() must stay pure — deriving a value with no side effects or signal mutations — you should prefer it over an effect() whenever you only need derived state, and you can model local component state with signals instead of manual change detection. Putting side effects or heavy work in a computed breaks its purity and memoization; side effects belong in effect().

questions.questionText: True or False: Using an effect() to read one signal and set() another signal is the recommended way to create derived state.
questions.explanation: deriving one signal's value from others is exactly what computed() is for — it is pure, memoized, and updates automatically. Using an effect() to read one signal and set another is an anti-pattern: it invites feedback loops, runs extra work, and Angular disallows writing to signals inside effects by default.
